Content from https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html:

asyncio.gather(*aws, return_exceptions=False)

Run awaitable objects in the aws sequence concurrently.

If any awaitable in aws is a coroutine, it is automatically scheduled as a Task.

If all awaitables are completed successfully, the result is an aggregate list of returned values. The order of result values corresponds to the order of awaitables in aws.

If return_exceptions is False (default), the first raised exception is immediately propagated to the task that awaits on gather(). Other awaitables in the aws sequence won't be cancelled and will continue to run.

If return_exceptions is True, exceptions are treated the same as successful results, and aggregated in the result list.

If gather() is cancelled, all submitted awaitables (that have not completed yet) are also cancelled.

Content from https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/configuration/:

# Configuring Ruff

Ruff reads configuration from pyproject.toml, ruff.toml, or .ruff.toml, in order of precedence.

## pyproject.toml example

    [tool.ruff]
    line-length = 100
    target-version = "py313"

    [tool.ruff.lint]
    select = ["E", "F", "I", "N", "UP", "B", "S", "A", "RUF"]
    ignore = ["E501"]
    unfixable = ["F401"]

    [tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]
    "tests/**/*.py" = ["S101", "D"]
    "scripts/*.py" = ["T201"]

    [tool.ruff.format]
    quote-style = "double"
    indent-style = "space"

Rule codes: E/W (pycodestyle), F (pyflakes), I (isort), N (pep8-naming), UP (pyupgrade), B (flake8-bugbear), S (bandit), RUF (ruff-specific). See the full list at https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/.

Content from https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/middleware/:

# Middleware

You can add middleware to FastAPI applications. A "middleware" is a function that works with every request before it is processed by any specific path operation, and also with every response before returning it.

## Create a middleware

    import time
    from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

    app = FastAPI()

    @app.middleware("http")
    async def add_process_time_header(request: Request, call_next):
        start_time = time.perf_counter()
        response = await call_next(request)
        process_time = time.perf_counter() - start_time
        response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
        return response

For full ASGI-level middleware, use app.add_middleware(StarletteMiddleware, ...). The @app.middleware decorator is the simplified shortcut. Technical detail: @app.middleware("http") is itself built on Starlette's BaseHTTPMiddleware.

Content from https://peps.python.org/pep-0654/:

PEP 654 – Exception Groups and except*

Status: Accepted. Final in Python 3.11.

Rationale: asyncio.TaskGroup and trio's nurseries can raise multiple exceptions concurrently. Before 3.11 there was no idiomatic way to either raise or handle them together.

ExceptionGroup / BaseExceptionGroup wrap a list of child exceptions. except* matches against the type of any child and handles that subset, leaving the rest to re-raise:

    try:
        ...
    except* ValueError as eg:
        handle_values(eg.exceptions)
    except* TypeError as eg:
        handle_types(eg.exceptions)

Note that plain except cannot catch an ExceptionGroup implicitly — you must use except ExceptionGroup or except*.

Content from https://nvidia.github.io/TensorRT-LLM/installation/build-from-source.html:

# Building TensorRT-LLM from source

For Blackwell consumer GPUs (sm_120), prebuilt wheels are not available. Build from source:

    git clone https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM.git
    cd TensorRT-LLM
    git submodule update --init --recursive
    export TRTLLM_CUDA_ARCHS="120-real"
    python ./scripts/build_wheel.py --trt_root /usr/local/tensorrt --clean

Requires: CUDA 12.8+, cuDNN 9.x, NCCL 2.20+, g++ 11+. Build wall time is 45–90 minutes on 16-core machines.

After build, install: `pip install ./build/tensorrt_llm-*.whl`.

Content from https://stackoverflow.com/questions/58223401/python-asyncio-gather-handle-exceptions:

Asked 4 years ago, modified 3 months ago. Viewed 180k times.

## Accepted answer

    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    for idx, result in enumerate(results):
        if isinstance(result, Exception):
            log.error("task %d failed: %r", idx, result)
        else:
            process(result)

This prevents one failure from cancelling siblings. If you want the first exception to cancel all in-flight tasks, use asyncio.wait(..., return_when=FIRST_EXCEPTION) instead. For Python 3.11+, prefer asyncio.TaskGroup which handles propagation via ExceptionGroup.

# Ruff

An extremely fast Python linter and code formatter, written in Rust.

Content from https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/:

# Pydantic Models

The primary way of defining schema in Pydantic is via models. Models are simply classes which inherit from BaseModel and define fields as annotated attributes.

    from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field

    class User(BaseModel):
        model_config = ConfigDict(strict=True, frozen=True, extra="forbid")

        id: int
        name: str = Field(min_length=1)
        email: str | None = None

## Model Configuration

- strict: disallow type coercion
- frozen: make instances immutable (hashable)
- extra="forbid": error on unknown fields (default "ignore")
- populate_by_name: allow setting via alias OR python name
- arbitrary_types_allowed: accept non-pydantic types without validators

Content from https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/queryguide/select.html:

# Writing SELECT statements for ORM Mapped Classes

New in 2.0: session.execute(select(...)).scalars() is the primary pattern. session.query() still exists but is "legacy".

    from sqlalchemy import select
    from sqlalchemy.orm import selectinload

    stmt = (
        select(User)
        .join(User.addresses)
        .where(Address.email_address.like("%@example.com"))
        .options(selectinload(User.addresses))
    )
    result = session.execute(stmt).scalars().unique().all()

Key changes from 1.x: .unique() is required for eager-loaded collections when using scalars(). .join() no longer requires an explicit ON clause when a single FK exists.

Content from https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/dockerfile_best-practices/:

# Dockerfile best practices

1. Use multi-stage builds to minimize final image size.
2. Pin base image by digest, not just tag.
3. Use `.dockerignore` to avoid leaking secrets.
4. Run as a non-root user; add USER 1000:1000 before CMD.
5. Drop capabilities with `--cap-drop=ALL` + `--cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE` as needed.
6. Use HEALTHCHECK so orchestrators can detect unhealthy containers.
7. Order layers by frequency-of-change — put requirements.txt before source code so pip cache survives code edits.

Content from https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html:

# torch.compile Troubleshooting

## Debugging graph breaks

    import torch
    fn = torch.compile(my_fn)
    explanation = torch._dynamo.explain(my_fn)(sample_input)
    print(explanation)

This prints the graph count, each break reason, the stack of the user code responsible, and the portion that was successfully traced.

## Common graph-break triggers

- .item() on tensors
- calling into numpy / ctypes
- non-tensor control flow that depends on tensor values
- Python builtins on non-Tensor sequences (e.g. list.pop during loss computation)

## Env flags

- TORCH_LOGS="+dynamo,graph_breaks" — prints breaks at runtime
- TORCHDYNAMO_VERBOSE=1 — stack traces with source lines
- mode="reduce-overhead" — also records CUDA graphs

Content from https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/histograms/:

# Histograms and summaries

Both histogram and summary compute quantiles on observations — but they differ fundamentally in WHERE the quantile is computed.

**Histogram** (recommended for most cases):
- Buckets are defined at instrumentation time.
- histogram_quantile() computes quantiles at query time from buckets.
- Can be aggregated across instances (sum by (le) (rate(bucket[5m]))).

**Summary**:
- Quantiles computed client-side, shipped pre-aggregated.
- CANNOT be aggregated across instances — averaging p99s is nonsense.
- Use only for single-instance apps, or when you truly need client-side quantiles with specific φ values.

## Picking buckets

Start with exponential buckets: 0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10.
The bucket just below your SLO should be a focus — that's what drives alerting precision.

Content from https://docs.trychroma.com/docs/collections/manage-collections:

# Managing collections

    import chromadb
    from chromadb.utils.embedding_functions import SentenceTransformerEmbeddingFunction

    client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")
    ef = SentenceTransformerEmbeddingFunction(model_name="BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")

    collection = client.get_or_create_collection(
        name="docs",
        embedding_function=ef,
        metadata={"hnsw:space": "cosine"},
    )

    collection.add(
        ids=["1", "2"],
        documents=["hello world", "goodbye world"],
        metadatas=[{"source": "a"}, {"source": "b"}],
    )

    results = collection.query(query_texts=["greeting"], n_results=5)

Note: the embedding_function is associated with the collection at create time and cannot be changed later. Attempting to do so raises an error.

Content from https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components:

# React Server Components

Server Components run on the server. They have no state, no effects, and no access to browser APIs, but they can:

- Fetch data directly with async/await
- Access the filesystem and environment variables
- Ship zero JavaScript to the browser

## Client Components

Files that start with "use client"; become the boundary between server and client code. Everything below "use client"; is bundled for the browser.

## Composition pattern

Pass server components as children or props to client components rather than importing them. This keeps server-only deps out of the client bundle.

    <ClientCarousel>{await fetchPosts()}</ClientCarousel>

Content from https://huggingface.co/docs/trl/v0.29.1/en/grpo_trainer:

# GRPOTrainer

Group Relative Policy Optimization trainer. Supports multiple loss formulations:

- loss_type="grpo" — original DeepSeekMath paper
- loss_type="bnpo" — beyond-policy batch norm
- loss_type="dapo" — direct advantage PO (recommended for small models)

## Config essentials

    GRPOConfig(
        output_dir="./runs",
        num_generations=8,
        max_prompt_length=512,
        max_completion_length=256,
        loss_type="dapo",
        beta=0.0,
        learning_rate=5e-6,
        bf16=True,          # NOT fp8 - fp8 is inference-only
        per_device_train_batch_size=1,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
    )

beta=0.0 with loss_type="dapo" disables the KL penalty — more stable on small (<4B) models that tend to collapse when KL dominates.

Content from https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/:

# LangGraph Core Concepts

## StateGraph

A graph of nodes operating on a shared state object. Edges determine flow; nodes mutate state.

    from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
    from typing_extensions import TypedDict

    class State(TypedDict):
        messages: list
        route: str

    graph = StateGraph(State)
    graph.add_node("classify", classify_fn)
    graph.add_node("search", search_fn)
    graph.add_node("answer", answer_fn)
    graph.add_conditional_edges("classify", lambda s: s["route"], {"rag": "search", "direct": "answer"})
    graph.add_edge("search", "answer")
    graph.add_edge("answer", END)
    graph.set_entry_point("classify")

## Checkpointers

Persist state between invocations. SqliteSaver and PostgresSaver are built-in. Enables human-in-the-loop via interrupts.

Content from https://github.com/NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM/releases:

# TensorRT-LLM v0.16.0 release notes

Released: 2026-03-20

## Highlights

- Native sm_120 (Blackwell consumer) FP8 kernels — 2.1–2.8x over vLLM fp8 on 70B-class models.
- EAGLE-2 speculative decoding support for Llama 3.x and Qwen 3.x.
- Improved GQA attention kernels; 18% throughput improvement on Llama-3.3-70B.
- New `trtllm-serve` CLI; OpenAI-compatible server built in.

## Breaking changes

- Minimum CUDA raised to 12.8. Drop support for sm_70 (V100).
- `builder.py` deprecated in favor of `convert_checkpoint.py` + `trtllm-build`.

## Upgrading from 0.15

Rebuild engines — engine format changed. Old .engine files will NOT load on 0.16.

POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls

## Parameters

- title (string, required): The title of the new pull request.
- head (string, required): The name of the branch where your changes are implemented.
- base (string, required): The name of the branch you want the changes pulled into.
- body (string): The contents of the pull request.
- draft (boolean): Indicates whether the pull request is a draft.
- maintainer_can_modify (boolean): Indicates whether maintainers can modify the pull request.

## Response 201 Created

Returns the full pull request object.
